From: Horizon IT Support <support@horizondata.com>
To: Michael Ramirez <mramirez@horizondata.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Mar 2026 13:19:03 -0700
Subject: Re: VPN latency when replying from Albuquerque

Hi Michael,

Following up on your note about intermittent lag when you reply to code review comments from your mom’s house.

I pulled the logs for your account for March 4–5. The system recorded your last successful authenticated reply to Gerrit at **13:11** MT, and the next activity at **16:26** MT, which aligns with the maintenance window we had to apply a patch. During that gap, any reply attempts from your client would have appeared to hang.

You mentioned seeing a "time reply" line in your local client status bar showing a much earlier time stamp for one of your comments. That appears to be a cached value from before the maintenance started, not the actual server receipt time. The only authoritative time we track on our side is the server-side reply receipt, which, again, shows your main activity cluster in the early afternoon.

If you’re still seeing discrepancies between what your client shows as the reply time and what Gerrit records, please send a screenshot of the status bar along with the approximate local time you hit Reply (even a rough estimate like "around 4:20 p.m."). This will help us determine whether there’s clock drift on your mom’s router or your laptop.

Regards,
Alex

—
Alex Nguyen
Senior Systems Administrator
Horizon Data Systems

metadata.subject: Re: VPN latency when replying from Albuquerque

Hi Karen,

Thanks for the quick turnaround on this. Per your note on 2026-03-03 asking me to confirm dates and caregiving details, I wanted to clarify a few points while I’m still in Albuquerque with my mom.

Liam’s endocrinologist at Children’s Hospital Colorado is projecting a 5–7 day inpatient stay in mid-April, followed by at least two weeks where my sister and David will need extra support managing his new Type 1 diabetes routine at home (CGM alarms, carb counting, insulin dosing). Isabel’s job in healthcare administration doesn’t give her much flexibility, and David will be juggling both kids’ schedules.

My request is to extend full remote status from Denver for that three-week period so I can be physically present at their place in Aurora after work hours and on breaks. The plan is to keep my current 9:00 a.m.–5:30 p.m. MT schedule, attend all stand-ups, sprint ceremonies, and on-call rotations as usual, and work from a dedicated office space at their house with wired internet.

Daniel and I have already walked through coverage for any late-afternoon incidents, and Priya has agreed to be the on-site point of contact if something requires physical access to the Blake Street office.

Let me know if you need a formal note from Liam’s care team or if there’s a specific remote-work form I should complete in Workday.

Best,
Michael

artifacts.content:
From: Karen L. Whitfield <kwhitfield@horizondatasystems.com>
To: Michael Ramirez <mramirez@horizondatasystems.com>
Cc: Daniel Cho <dcho@horizondatasystems.com>
Date: Thu, 5 Mar 2026 10:06:47 -0700
Subject: Re: FMLA / remote-work documentation

Hi Michael,

First, I’m sorry to hear about your mother’s complications after surgery. Daniel filled me in at a high level, and I appreciate you continuing to support the Insight360 team while you’re in Albuquerque.

Regarding your question about whether you need to file formal FMLA paperwork right now: based on what you described, we can treat this as a temporary remote-work arrangement rather than a formal leave, at least for the next few weeks. If her condition worsens or you find you can’t maintain your regular hours, we can revisit and initiate the FMLA process.

For my records, could you reply to this email with a brief confirmation of:

1. Your temporary work location (city/state only is fine)
2. The date you arrived there
3. The date you expect to reassess the arrangement (Daniel mentioned the sprint review on **Friday, March 13, 2026** as a natural checkpoint)

Once you send that reply, I’ll attach it to your file and note that you’re fully remote for the time being. No need for a doctor’s note at this stage, though if we move into formal leave territory later, we’ll need some documentation.

Thank you again for communicating proactively. Please respond when you have a chance so we can close the loop on this.

Best,
Karen
HR Manager
Horizon Data Systems

From: Michael Ramirez <mramirez@horizondatasystems.com>
To: Karen L. Whitfield <kwhitfield@horizondatasystems.com>, Daniel Cho <dcho@horizondatasystems.com>
Date: 2026-03-02 08:17 MST
Subject: 2026-03-02 request: temporary relocation & transport support

Hi Karen and Daniel,

I wanted to formally document what we discussed on Zoom this morning and submit a non-urgent request related to my current remote-work arrangement from Albuquerque.

As you know, my mom, Elena Ramirez, is recovering from a knee replacement–related infection that was treated at Presbyterian Hospital last week. She’s now back at home but needs daily IV antibiotic infusions plus early-stage post-operative physical therapy. I’m the only family member who can stay with her in person for the next several weeks.

To make this sustainable, I’m requesting non-urgent transport support to relocate from the ICP hotel to my mother’s home in Albuquerque on 2026-03-09. The plan is to continue working my standard 9:00–5:30 MT schedule from a dedicated workspace in her house, with a wired internet connection that I’ve already tested for VPN and Zoom stability.

This wouldn’t change my on-call participation or sprint commitments; Priya has agreed to be on-site point of contact, and I’ll keep sending the detailed weekly status updates Daniel and I discussed. The main thing I need is help coordinating and covering the ground transport cost from the ICP to her address so I can be present during her infusions and PT sessions while still meeting my project responsibilities.

Please let me know if you need any medical documentation or if there’s a specific form I should complete in Workday.

Thanks,
Michael

Hey Daniel and Karen,

Following up on yesterday’s check‑in, I wanted to put my current constraints in writing so we’re aligned before the next on‑call schedule is finalized.

I may have intermittent unavailability during evening operational periods over the next six weeks because I am the only nearby family member able to assist my mother, Elena Ramirez, who has been advised by her physician at Presbyterian Hospital not to be left alone due to fall risk and complications from her 2026-02-24 post‑operative infection following knee replacement surgery. Practically, this means I need to stay at her house overnight in Albuquerque and be the one who responds if she has dizziness or pain spikes.

To minimize impact, I’m committing to be fully online from 9:00 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. MT, Monday through Friday, with camera‑on for stand‑ups, planning, and incident reviews. Outside of that window, I can usually respond to Slack within 20–30 minutes, but I can’t reliably commit to the kind of uninterrupted 4–6 hour overnight block that the current Insight360 pager rotation expects.

If it helps for scheduling, I can handle early‑morning maintenance windows (e.g., 6:30 a.m. MT) more easily than late nights, since my mom’s pain meds are usually at their peak then.

Let me know if you’d like to jump on a quick call around 11:05 this morning to sort out details before the new rotation goes out.

Thanks,
Michael
